title: Deep Litter Method: Easy, Low-Odor Coop Bedding for Homestead Flocks

blocks.text: The deep litter method turns your coop floor into a living compost pile. Add fresh bedding on top of the old stuff and let natural microbes break it down. It stays dry, controls odor, and gives you free garden fertilizer.
Most homesteaders only clean out the coop 1–2 times a year instead of every week. Perfect for busy keepers with 10–50 birds.

blocks.title: How to start deep litter
items: Cover the floor with 4–6 inches of pine shavings or hemp bedding
Add a thin layer of leaves, straw, or garden waste for carbon balance
Sprinkle a handful of DE or wood ash every few weeks to control moisture
Scatter a little scratch grain daily so the birds turn the pile for you

blocks.title: Pro tip
blocks.text: The secret is keeping the litter dry. If it feels damp, add another 2 inches of fresh bedding and stir.

blocks.title: Watch for moisture
blocks.text: Wet litter breeds ammonia and harmful bacteria. In humid climates, add extra ventilation or switch to sand in the run area.

blocks.title: Monthly maintenance checklist
items: Stir the top 4 inches with a garden rake
Add 2–3 inches of fresh bedding when it compacts
Remove any very wet or caked spots immediately
Harvest finished compost from the bottom layer in spring and fall

blocks.title: Bonus benefit
blocks.text: Your birds get natural grit and bugs from the working litter while you get rich compost for the garden — win-win!

title: Chicken Mites & Lice: Detection, Treatment & Prevention

blocks.text: Mites and lice are tiny but can stress your flock fast. Early detection keeps problems small and prevents egg production drops or anemia.

blocks.title: Quick detection checklist
items: Check vents and under wings weekly for tiny moving specks or white nits
Look for pale combs, restlessness at night, or excessive preening
Shake a white sheet under a roost at night — red mites show up as red dots

blocks.title: Don’t wait
blocks.text: Heavy infestations can kill young birds or stop laying entirely. Treat the whole flock at the same time.

blocks.title: Safe treatment steps
items: Give every bird a thorough dust bath with food-grade diatomaceous earth or wood ash mix
Spray coop cracks and roosts with neem oil or diluted permethrin (follow label exactly)
Replace all nesting material and wash perches with hot soapy water
Repeat treatment in 7–10 days to catch newly hatched eggs

blocks.title: Prevention is easier
blocks.text: A deep dust-bath area with play sand + DE + sulfur keeps most parasites away naturally. Add fresh herbs like lavender and mint to nesting boxes as a bonus repellent.

title: How to Introduce New Chickens to Your Existing Flock (Without Bloodshed)

blocks.text: Chickens take their pecking order seriously. A proper introduction prevents injuries and keeps everyone calm.

blocks.title: Quarantine first
blocks.text: Always isolate new birds for at least 14–30 days and watch for illness. This step alone prevents most flock disasters.

blocks.title: Safe introduction checklist
items: Place the new birds in a separate pen inside the run so they can see each other
Swap bedding and feeders for a few days so smells mix
Introduce them in the evening when everyone is roosting
Provide 2–3 extra feeding stations and waterers to reduce competition
Watch for 48 hours and separate any bird that’s being bullied

blocks.title: Pro tip
blocks.text: Add a few extra perches and hiding spots (old boards or crates) so lower-ranking birds can get away. The flock usually settles in 3–7 days.

title: Broody Hens: How to Break a Broody or Let Her Hatch Eggs Safely

blocks.text: A broody hen stops laying and sits on eggs 24/7. You can break the broodiness quickly or turn her into a natural incubator.

blocks.title: Spot a broody hen
items: She stays in the nest box all day and night
She puffs up, growls, or pecks when you reach in
She plucks her own breast feathers to line the nest

blocks.title: How to break a broody (3–5 days)
items: Move her to a wire-bottom cage with food and water but no nesting material
Keep her in bright light and cool air for 3–5 days
Return her to the main coop once she starts laying again

blocks.title: Let her hatch instead
blocks.text: Give her 8–12 fertile eggs, mark the calendar, and leave her alone. She’ll handle turning, humidity, and chick care better than any incubator.

blocks.title: Health check
blocks.text: Broody hens lose weight fast. Make sure she’s eating and drinking daily. If she stays broody longer than 3 weeks without eggs, consult a vet.

title: Predator-Proof Your Coop & Run: Simple Steps That Actually Work

blocks.text: Raccoons, foxes, hawks, and dogs are smart. A few cheap upgrades can stop almost every nighttime raid.

blocks.title: Must-have predator-proof upgrades
items: Bury hardware cloth 12–18 inches deep around the entire run
Use ½-inch hardware cloth on all windows and vents (not chicken wire)
Install automatic coop doors that close at dusk
Lock all doors with carabiner clips — raccoons can open latches
Cover the top of runs with bird netting or welded wire if hawks are a problem

blocks.title: Never rely on lights alone
blocks.text: Motion lights and radios help, but determined predators ignore them. Combine with solid barriers.

blocks.title: Pro tip
blocks.text: Walk around your coop at night with a flashlight once a month. Any gap big enough for two fingers is big enough for a weasel.

blocks.title: Free-range bonus
blocks.text: Bring birds in before dusk and use guardian animals (dogs, geese, or guineas) for daytime protection.

title: Beak Trimming (Debeaking) for Backyard Chickens: When It’s Needed & Better Alternatives

blocks.text: Beak trimming (also called debeaking) is the partial removal of the sharp tip of a chicken’s upper beak—sometimes the lower beak too. It’s done to reduce feather pecking, cannibalism, and egg eating, especially when birds are kept in close quarters.
On a homestead with good space and management, most keepers never need to trim beaks. Many small-flock owners successfully avoid it entirely by focusing on prevention instead of correction.

blocks.title: Welfare first
blocks.text: Beak trimming causes acute pain and can lead to long-term sensitivity or neuromas. It also interferes with natural foraging and preening. Many countries are phasing it out in favor of better housing and enrichment.

blocks.title: Prevention checklist – stop pecking before it starts
items: Give each bird at least 4 sq ft in the coop and 10 sq ft in the run
Add enrichment: pecking blocks, hanging cabbage, mirrors, or dust-bath areas
Use dim or red lighting in the coop to reduce stress and aggression
Feed a balanced layer ration plus free-choice oyster shell and grit
Provide plenty of fresh water and scatter treats to keep birds busy
Separate any overly aggressive birds immediately

blocks.title: Pro tip from homesteaders
blocks.text: A bored flock pecks. Ten minutes of free-range time or a fresh pile of garden greens each day often calms the whole group faster than any other fix.

If serious pecking or cannibalism continues despite prevention, some experienced keepers choose to trim beaks as a last resort. Only do this if you’re confident in the technique or have help from a vet or seasoned poultry friend.

blocks.title: Safe trimming checklist (experienced keepers only)
items: Best age: 1–10 days old (fastest healing) or 8–12 weeks
Use a sharp, sanitized hot-blade trimmer or professional tool
Remove no more than ⅓ of the upper beak tip
Cauterize immediately to stop bleeding
Offer soft mash feed and extra vitamins K & C for 3–5 days afterward
Provide easy-access water and monitor eating for 48 hours

blocks.title: Risks of improper trimming
blocks.text: Cutting too deep can cause bleeding, infection, refusal to eat, or death. If you’re unsure, consult a veterinarian or skip it and improve housing instead.

blocks.title: Long-term flock health tip
blocks.text: Healthy, enriched birds with room to roam rarely need beak trimming. Focus on management and you’ll keep both your conscience and your flock happy.

title: Supplemental Lighting for Egg Production: Bulbs, Timers & Red Lights

blocks.text: Short winter days kill egg production fast. Adding 14–16 total hours of light per day keeps your layers laying without forcing a stressful molt.
A simple timer + the right bulb is all most backyard flocks need. Red lights add an extra calm factor that many homesteaders swear by.

blocks.title: Lighting setup checklist
items: Install a heavy-duty outdoor timer on a dedicated circuit
Use one 40–60 W equivalent LED or incandescent bulb per 100 sq ft of coop floor
Hang lights high and off to the side so birds aren’t staring directly into them
Start adding light gradually in early fall (15–30 min extra per week)
Switch to red bulbs if you notice pecking or aggression

blocks.title: Fire & safety first
blocks.text: Never leave heat lamps on overnight for lighting — use cool LED bulbs only. Always use outdoor-rated cords and a GFCI outlet in the coop.

blocks.title: Why red light works so well
blocks.text: Chickens see red poorly, so a red bulb feels like “dim daylight” to them. It stimulates laying while reducing stress and cannibalism at the same time.

title: Chicken Feed Types, Forms & Daily Feeding Amounts

blocks.text: Complete feeds are balanced for each life stage. Offer them free-choice with fresh water and grit (after 2–3 weeks). Treats and scratch should stay under 10% of the diet.

blocks.title: Life-stage feed comparison
items: Chick Starter (0–6/8 weeks): 18–24% protein, low calcium – builds fast growth
Grower/Pullet (6–20 weeks): 14–18% protein – steady frame development
Layer (18+ weeks): 15–18% protein, 3–4% calcium – strong shells and eggs
Broiler Finisher (final weeks): higher energy for meat birds

blocks.title: Daily feed per bird (free-choice)
items: 0–2 weeks starter: 0.5–1 oz
2–4 weeks starter: 1–1.5 oz
4–8 weeks starter: 1.5–2.5 oz
8–20 weeks grower: 2.5–4 oz
18+ weeks layer: 3.5–5 oz

blocks.title: For your 25-bird flock
blocks.text: Weeks 1–2: very low intake. By weeks 5–8 you’ll go through 15–25+ lb of starter weekly. Adjust for breed, heat, and free-ranging—healthy birds with full crops at night are on track.

title: Starter Feed for Baby Chicks: Timing, Quantities & Storage

blocks.text: Baby chicks need high-protein starter feed (18–22%) for the first 6–8 weeks until they’re fully feathered and look like little chickens. After that, switch to grower feed to avoid excess protein.

blocks.title: How much to buy for 25 chicks
items: Expect roughly 6–8 lb of starter per chick over the full period
Total for your flock: 150–200 lb (don’t buy it all at once)
Start with one or two 25–50 lb bags for weeks 1–4
Add a 50 lb bag for the rest—most backyarders find this lasts 4–6 weeks

blocks.title: Feed storage in humid climates
blocks.text: In warm, humid areas use it within 3–4 months max. Store in airtight metal or heavy plastic bins off the ground, cool and dry. Toss any musty, moldy, or buggy feed immediately.

blocks.title: Pro tip
blocks.text: Always offer free-choice starter so chicks self-regulate. Scatter a little on paper towels the first day to help weak eaters learn fast.

title: Red Heat Lamps for Brooding Chicks

blocks.text: The light bulb in your brooder—usually a red or white heat lamp—keeps day-old chicks warm because they can’t regulate their own body temperature yet. A steady 95–100°F (35–38°C) under the lamp for the first week mimics a mother hen and prevents piling, chilling, or death.

blocks.title: Temperature matters most
blocks.text: Start at 95°F directly under the lamp and drop 5°F each week. Always watch chick behavior—huddling under the lamp means too cold; spreading out and panting means too hot.

blocks.title: Why red light is the homestead favorite
items: Delivers radiant heat while letting chicks rest at night
Reduces pecking and cannibalism by masking blood and calming the flock
Lowers stress and aggression in dense brooders
Stimulates reproductive hormones later for earlier lay in pullets

blocks.title: Pro tip
blocks.text: Use a red heat lamp 18–24 inches above the brooder floor. Combine with free-choice starter feed and check temperatures twice daily—your calm, evenly spread chicks will thank you.
The first week sets the pace for healthy growth. Keep chicks warm, dry, and eating within the first 24 hours after arrival.

blocks.title: Critical temperature range
blocks.text: Start at 95°F (35°C) directly under the heat source, then reduce by 5°F each week. Watch chick behavior instead of relying only on a thermometer.

blocks.title: Daily checklist
items: Refresh water twice daily and clean drinkers
Provide chick starter feed at all times
Inspect vents for pasting and clean gently if needed
Replace damp bedding immediately

blocks.title: Pro tip
blocks.text: Scatter a small amount of feed on paper towels on day one so weak eaters learn quickly.

title: Feeding Layers for Better Egg Output

blocks.text: Consistent feed quality and timing improve shell strength and production. Sudden feed changes can reduce laying for several days.

blocks.title: Nutrition routine
items: Use 16% to 18% layer ration as baseline
Offer oyster shell free-choice for calcium
Provide insoluble grit if birds range less
Avoid feeding scratch as more than 10% of diet

blocks.title: Mycotoxin risk
blocks.text: Store feed dry and elevated off concrete. Moldy feed can cause severe drops in egg production and bird health issues.
Simple barriers and routines reduce disease introduction. Keep outside traffic low and isolate new birds before mixing.

blocks.title: Core controls
items: Quarantine new birds for at least 14 days
Use dedicated boots for coop area
Disinfect crates and tools after shared use
Limit visitors near birds and feeders

blocks.title: Pro tip
blocks.text: Create a handwashing point near the coop entrance to improve compliance.
Egg production often declines in high heat. Focus on airflow, cool water, and shade during the hottest hours.

blocks.title: Emergency signs
blocks.text: Panting with wings spread and lethargy can indicate heat stress. Immediate cooling action is required.

blocks.title: Heat plan
items: Refill cool water several times per day
Add shade cloth over exposed runs
Increase coop airflow with safe fans
Offer wet mash or water-rich treats in evening

blocks.text: Handle eggs gently and cool them quickly for best quality. Collection timing affects cleanliness and crack rates.

blocks.title: Collection standards
items: Collect at least twice daily
Discard heavily cracked eggs
Dry clean first; wet wash only when needed
Store point-down in cartons
